# The Last Bus

The 11:47 became my compass point, the one reliable thing in a year that refused to cohere.

I'd moved to the city in September, subletting a room from someone I didn't know in a neighborhood I couldn't pronounce. My job ended at ten, which meant I walked forty minutes to the bus stop because the closer station closed for repairs and stayed closed, a half-finished project behind chain-link fencing. By the time I reached the corner of Morrison and 18th, it was usually 11:40. I'd stand under the awning of the closed pharmacy and wait.

The first month, I memorized the schedule without trying. The 11:47. The 12:22. The 12:58, though I only took that one twice, both times after missing the earlier bus by seconds, watching its red taillights disappear around the corner while I stood on the curb, defeated.

You learn a city differently at night. The bus route home—forty-three minutes if traffic was light, longer if there was construction or an accident or the driver was in no particular hurry—passed through three neighborhoods I never visited during daylight. A strip club with blacked-out windows. A twenty-four-hour laundromat where I once saw a woman folding what must have been a hundred shirts, methodical and alone. A church with a marquee that changed messages monthly: *GOD SEES YOU*. *FORGIVENESS IS FREE*. *TRY PRAYER WHEN YOU'RE ANGRY*.

The regulars settled into their seats like claims staked on land. The woman with the crochet hook and the enormous tote bag, always working on something I never saw finished. The man in the wheelchair who boarded at 12th and talked to himself in a low, rhythmic murmur that almost sounded like singing. The young couple who got on at the university stop, he in scrubs, she in a restaurant uniform, both of them exhausted in that particular way of people working their way toward something better. They held hands even when they fell asleep.

I sat in the fifth row, aisle seat. I don't know why that one. It felt right—close enough to the door, far enough from the front-wheel hump that made your knees too high. I read books on my phone, the glow of the screen making me visible to anyone who cared to look, which no one did. We were all engaged in the same small performance of privacy, pretending we weren't sharing this metal box hurtling through the dark.

November brought rain that turned the windows into mirrors. I watched the city slide past as a smear of color, neon signs bleeding into streetlights bleeding into the headlights of passing cars. The bus smelled different then—wet wool, damp newspaper, the particular mustiness of coats that never quite dried. People were grimmer, less likely to nod hello. We were all just trying to get somewhere warm.

There was a driver named Carl who worked Tuesdays and Thursdays. He called everyone "boss" or "sweetheart" depending on some calculus I couldn't determine. He drove fast but stopped long, holding the doors for people running to catch us. Once, in January, he waited a full thirty seconds for an elderly man making his slow way up the block, leaning heavily on a cane. When the man finally boarded, breathless and apologizing, Carl just said, "Take your time, sweetheart. We ain't going nowhere without you."

I wrote in a notebook sometimes, trying to capture something of what these rides felt like. But the pages just filled with observations that seemed meaningful in the moment and banal later: *Man in gray coat, same as yesterday. Woman crying quietly, three seats back. Driver forgot to announce the transfer point.* The notebook filled, and I started another, and still I couldn't say what I was trying to record. The texture of a life, maybe. The way time moves when you're in between places.

Spring came late. March was cold, April colder. But eventually the nights softened, and people started sitting near the windows again, letting in the smell of rain-warm asphalt and new leaves. The woman with the crochet hook switched from thick wool to cotton thread, her projects turning lacy and light. The couple from the university stop stopped holding hands—I noticed that. They sat apart, she looking out the window, he staring at his phone. One night in May, she wasn't there. He rode alone, his uniform wrinkled, his face slack with something that looked like grief or maybe just exhaustion.

I should have talked to people. I know that now. A year of sharing this small space, and I never learned anyone's name. Not Carl's, though I could have asked. Not the crochet woman's, though she smiled at me once when I dropped my wallet and she picked it up. Not the man in the wheelchair, though he said "thank you" every time someone held the door, his voice gentle and surprised, as if kindness still caught him off guard.

But that's the strange intimacy of public transit, or maybe just of cities: you can know someone by their silhouette, their cough, the particular way they settle into a molded plastic seat, without knowing anything about them at all. We were fellow travelers in the most literal sense, bound together by route and schedule, by the particular loneliness of late-night passage.

By summer, I'd stopped noticing the details. The ride had become transparent, a non-event between the end of work and the beginning of home. I read my books. I watched the darkened storefronts. I counted the stops—fourteen between my boarding and my exit—and sometimes I fell asleep and missed one, jerking awake to find myself two blocks past where I should have been.

The last time I rode the 11:47 was late August. I'd found a different job, closer to home, with hours that let me take the train. I stood at Morrison and 18th for the final time, and I felt something I couldn't name. Not sadness exactly. Not relief. Just the quiet recognition that a chapter had closed without my noticing, that I'd lived through something that was already becoming memory.

The bus arrived, right on time. Carl was driving. "Hey, boss," he said, and I nodded, and I took my seat in the fifth row, aisle side. The crochet woman was three rows back, working on something in pale blue. The man in the wheelchair hummed his low song. At the university stop, the young man boarded alone, still wearing scrubs, still looking tired.

I watched the city pass. The strip club, the laundromat, the church with its new message: *YOU ARE LOVED MORE THAN YOU KNOW*. I thought about taking a picture, but that would have been sentimental, and anyway the light was wrong.

Forty-three minutes. Fourteen stops. A year of my life measured in distance traveled after dark, in the small kindnesses of strangers who remained strangers, in the particular solitude of being one among many, all of us going home.

I pulled the cord for my stop. The bus sighed to a halt. I stepped onto the sidewalk and didn't look back, though I heard the doors close behind me, heard the engine strain as we pulled away into the night.

# The Deep End

The Maplewood Pool opened on Memorial Day and closed on Labor Day, and in the ten weeks between, you could watch the entire town pass through its chain-link gates.

I worked there for four summers, starting when I was sixteen. My job was technically "pool attendant," which meant I checked membership cards, fished leaves out of the water, and occasionally blew a whistle at children running on the concrete deck. But really, I watched people.

The pool sat on the edge of town, carved into a hillside behind the recreation center. It wasn't fancy—no water slides or lazy rivers, just a rectangular lap pool, a shallow end for kids, and a grassy area with faded umbrellas. The locker rooms smelled permanently of chlorine and damp concrete. The snack bar sold soft pretzels and ice cream sandwiches at prices that hadn't changed since the 1990s.

What the pool revealed, more than anything, was who belonged.

Maplewood was the kind of town that liked to think of itself as integrated, progressive, diverse. The school district boasted about its demographics. The downtown had a co-op grocery store and a fair-trade coffee shop. But the pool showed the seams.

You had to buy a membership to get in. A family pass cost $150 for the summer, which wasn't nothing but wasn't prohibitive either. Still, the crowd was self-selecting

# The Captain of the Narrow Sea

Every morning at 5:45, Harold Pence walks down the wooden dock at Miller's Landing, his boots making the same sound they've made for thirty years—a hollow thump that the gulls have learned to ignore. The ferry *Matilda* waits in the dark water, her white paint faded to the color of old bone, and Harold boards her the way a man climbs into his own skin.

The route between Miller's Landing and Cooper's Reach is not a journey that appears on most maps. Fourteen nautical miles across what the locals call the Narrow Sea, a bite of water that separates two towns that might have been one town if not for a stubborn argument about the post office in 1892. The crossing takes forty-seven minutes when the weather holds. Harold has made it 21,842 times, give or take.

"You don't get bored," he says, settling into the wheelhouse as the first light bleeds gray over the eastern shore. "People ask me that. They say, same water, same towns, same boat. But it's never the same. The water's different every day. The light's different. The passengers are different, even when they're the same people."

He pulls the throttle back and *Matilda* shudders awake beneath him. At 6:00 exactly, she pulls away from the dock.

# The Weight of 187 Files

Marta Reyes keeps a mental tally of her clients. Not by name—there are too many for that—but by the shape of their trouble. The shape changes, but the weight stays the same.

It's 7:45 a.m. on a Tuesday in the public defender's office of Millbrook County, a midsize jurisdiction in the industrial Midwest. Reyes has been at her desk for forty minutes. Before noon, she will meet with six clients, argue two bail hearings, and review a plea offer that could mean the difference between probation and three years in state prison. She will not eat breakfast. She will forget to call her mother back. She will drive home in the dark and think about the one she couldn't save, the one whose face she can't quite remember anymore.

Her caseload currently stands at 187 open files.

---

The public defender's office occupies the third floor of a concrete building across the street from the courthouse, connected by a pedestrian bridge that everyone calls the Bridge of Sorrows, though no one remembers who coined the phrase. The carpets are gray, the walls are beige, the filing cabinets are army surplus from a government auction in 1987. The office smells like stale coffee and the particular desperation of bureaucratic inertia.

Reyes, forty-three, has worked here for eleven years. She grew up three miles from where she now sits, daughter of a factory worker and a school secretary, first in her family to attend college, then law school at the state university, then back home because the debt was real and the idealism was realer. She planned to stay two years. Two years became five. Five became eleven

At 6:45 AM, the hallways of Martin Luther King Jr. High School are a cathedral of echoes. The only sound is the rhythmic *swish-thud* of a push broom against linoleum, a metronome marking the final moments of silence before the chaos of the day begins. This is the domain of Elias "Mr. E" Thorne, a man who has held the keys to the building longer than the current principal has been out of college.

For 28 years, Thorne has walked these floors. He was here when the library was carpeted in shag, and he was here when they tore it out for hardwood. He watched the smartboards replace the chalkboards, and he watched the graduating class of 1999 return for their twentieth reunion, slightly grayer and wider around the middle. In a profession defined by turnover—where principals rotate every few years and teachers burn out or move districts—Thorne is the geological constant.

But it isn’t his longevity that makes him a fixture; it is his memory.

By the time the first bell rings at 7:20 AM, Thorne is usually stationed by the main entrance, a yellow vest over his gray work shirt, a ring of keys jangling at his hip. As the flood of teenagers pours in—a river of hoodies, headphones, and backpacks—he does not just monitor the crowd. He greets it.

"Jacket on, Marcus, it’s freezing in the science wing today," he calls out, his voice gravelly from decades of inhaling cleaning chemicals and school dust.

A sophomore boy in a varsity jacket looks up, startled, then grins. "Got it, Mr. E."

"Keisha, how’s that history project coming? Your mom said you were up late."

"I'm almost done, Mr. Thorne!" a girl shouts back without breaking her stride.

Administrators estimate the school houses roughly 1,400 students. Thorne knows 1,400 names. He does not need a seating chart or a yearbook. He knows them because he cleans up after them, yes, but mostly because he sees them. He sees the freshman who cries in the bathroom during third period, the couple sneaking a kiss behind the gym bleachers, and the quiet kid who stays late to avoid going home. He knows their siblings, often recalling the specific messes their older brothers or sisters made a decade prior
